final_message:
All five functions are implemented in `problems.py` and every check passes, including a 1M-element performance run for `rolling_max` (0.39s).

How each is implemented:

- **`parse_duration`** — a single anchored regex `(?:([0-9]+)h)?(?:([0-9]+)m)?(?:([0-9]+)s)?` via `fullmatch`, requiring at least one group. This structurally rejects out-of-order units, duplicates, bare units, negatives, whitespace, and trailing garbage; `[0-9]` (not `\d`) also rejects Unicode digits, and non-string input raises `ValueError` up front.
- **`luhn_valid`** — strips spaces/hyphens, returns `False` for empty results, non-strings, or anything outside ASCII `0-9`, then runs the standard right-to-left Luhn doubling. `"0"` passes (sum 0), other single digits fail.
- **`flatten`** — recursive walk building joined keys with `sep`, using a `None` prefix sentinel so an empty-string top-level key still joins correctly. Empty nested dicts contribute nothing, non-dict values (lists included) pass through untouched, and the input is never mutated.
- **`top_k_frequent`** — `Counter` plus one sort keyed on `(-count, word)`; returns `[]` for `k <= 0` and all distinct words when `k` exceeds them.
- **`rolling_max`** — monotonic deque of indices (O(n) total), `ValueError` for `window <= 0`, `[]` when `window > len(nums)`.

My throwaway test covered all docstring examples, the listed error cases, a randomized brute-force cross-check for `rolling_max` (300 cases), and the timing test — everything passed.
